Developed an automated PowerBI reporting system using SQL, later adopted division-wide

title: Projects
text:
A selection of data science projects showcasing statistical modeling, Bayesian inference, and interactive visualization.


An interactive application comparing two Bayesian volatility models on S&P 500 weekly data (2013–2023). Built with Shiny for Python, featuring real-time model comparison and Plotly visualizations.
Highlights:

Bayesian parameter estimation using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (Stan)
GARCH(1,1) vs Stochastic Volatility model comparison
Out-of-sample prediction including the COVID-19 volatility spike
Object-oriented Python design with abstract classes, inheritance, and polymorphism

View Project Details →

title: About
text:
I’m a Data Science and Economics student at Central European University, graduating in June 2026. I spent my third year at the London School of Economics studying Stochastic and Actuarial Methods in Finance, Macroeconomics, and Valuation & Hedging — earning a Distinction.
My interests sit at the intersection of quantitative finance, macroeconomics, and data science. I’ve worked at Goldman Sachs (Equities Market Risk) and Raiffeisen Bank International, and I’m joining a macro-focused hedge fund in Abu Dhabi as an Investment Associate after graduation.



Programming: Python, SQL, Stata
Statistics & ML: Bayesian inference, time series analysis, volatility modeling, econometrics, machine learning
Tools: Bloomberg, Refinitiv, Excel, PowerBI, Git, Databricks, Google Analytics
Frameworks: Shiny, Quarto, Jupyter, Stan

title: Home
text:
Welcome to my Data Science portfolio. I’m a final-year BSc Data Science and Economics student at Central European University, with a study abroad year at the London School of Economics. My interests lie in quantitative finance, macro investing, and Bayesian statistics.
Previously at Goldman Sachs (Equities Market Risk) and Raiffeisen Bank International. Incoming Macro Investment Associate at a hedge fund in Abu Dhabi.


Bayesian Volatility Modeling — Comparing GARCH(1,1) and Stochastic Volatility models for predicting S&P 500 weekly volatility using Bayesian inference with Stan. This project demonstrates how prior beliefs and posterior sampling lead to more robust parameter estimates than classical maximum likelihood.
View Project →

title: Bayesian Volatility Modeling
text:
This project applies Bayesian inference to estimate and compare two volatility models on S&P 500 (IVV ETF) weekly data. The models were trained on 2013–2017 data and evaluated on 2018–present, capturing events like the COVID-19 market crash.


GARCH(1,1) models conditional variance as a deterministic function of past squared shocks and past variance:
\[\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 a_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2\]
Stochastic Volatility (SV) treats log-volatility as a latent AR(1) process, allowing for smoother, continuous changes:
\[y_t = \epsilon_t \exp\left(\frac{h_t}{2}\right), \quad h_{t+1} = \mu + \phi(h_t - \mu) + \delta_t \sigma\]



Instead of finding a single “best” parameter set via maximum likelihood, Bayesian inference computes the full posterior distribution of parameters using MCMC sampling (Stan). This gives more robust estimates and naturally quantifies uncertainty.



Explore the model results below — switch between training and test data, toggle models on and off, and compare their predictive accuracy.

Replace the iframe URL above with your Posit Connect Cloud deployment link.




The SV model achieved lower MSE on both training and test data overall
GARCH(1,1) better captured the COVID-19 volatility spike — its deterministic structure reacts quickly to large shocks
SV performed better during stable periods — its latent process provides smoother, more accurate estimates when volatility is low
Both models benefit from Bayesian estimation, producing robust parameter estimates even with relatively short training periods




The Shiny application is built with object-oriented principles:

Abstract Base Classes: DataLoader and VolatilityModel define interfaces
Inheritance: CSVDataLoader, GARCHModel, SVModel implement specific behavior
Polymorphism: The Visualizer works with any VolatilityModel without knowing its type
Encapsulation: DataManager hides data loading behind property accessors
Composition: AppController wires all components together (Facade pattern)




pip install -r requirements.txt
cd projects/data
shiny run app.py
Then visit http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.




Python 3.9+ — core language
Shiny for Python — interactive web framework
Plotly — interactive visualizations
pandas / NumPy — data manipulation
Stan (PyStan) — Bayesian MCMC sampling (used for training, not in the app)
ArviZ — Bayesian diagnostics (used for analysis, not in the app)
